questions.question: What is the key difference between visibility: hidden and display: none?
choices: visibility: hidden hides the element but it still takes up space; display: none removes it from the flow entirely
display: none hides the element but it still takes up space; visibility: hidden removes it from the flow
visibility: hidden only works on inline elements
questions.explanation: visibility: hidden makes the element invisible but it still occupies its space in the layout. display: none removes the element from the document flow entirely, as if it does not exist.

questions.question: Which display value makes an element behave like a block element but flow inline with text?
questions.explanation: inline-block elements flow inline with surrounding text like inline elements, but they accept width, height, and vertical margin/padding like block elements.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about inline elements?
choices: They always start on a new line
Width and height properties have no effect on them
They create a new stacking context
questions.explanation: Inline elements do not start on a new line and only take up as much width as necessary. The width and height properties have no effect on inline elements.

questions.question: What does display: flex do to an element?
choices: Makes the element an inline element
Establishes the element as a flex container for its children
Makes the element stretch to fill the viewport
questions.explanation: display: flex turns an element into a block-level flex container, enabling flexbox layout for its direct children (flex items).

questions.question: Setting display: ___ removes an element from the document flow entirely, as if it does not exist.
questions.explanation: display: none removes the element from the layout completely. It takes up no space and is not rendered, though it remains in the DOM.

questions.question: The ___ display value makes an element a block-level container that lays out children on a two-dimensional grid.
questions.explanation: display: grid establishes a grid formatting context for the element's children, enabling two-dimensional layout with rows and columns.
